content:
# Galatians Chapter 2 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/GAL_002_page_001.jpg

1. Then after fourteen years I went up again to Hierosolyma with Barnabas, having taken along also Titos.

2. And I went up according to a revelation. And I set before them the gospel which I proclaim among the nations, but privately to the [ones] of repute, lest somehow I should be running or had run in vain.

3. But not even Titos, the [one] with me, being a Greek, was compelled to be circumcised —

4. but because of the false brothers, secretly brought in, who came in secretly to spy out our freedom which we have in Christos Yeshua, that they might enslave us —

5. to whom not even for an hour did we yield in subjection, that the truth of the gospel might remain with you.

6. But from the [ones] reputed to be something — whatever they formerly were makes no difference to me; God does not receive [the] face of a man — for to me the [ones] of repute added nothing.

7. But on the contrary, having seen that I have been entrusted [with] the gospel of the uncircumcision, just as Petros [with that] of the circumcision —

8. for the [one] having worked in Petros for [the] apostleship of the circumcision worked also in me for the nations —

9. and having known the grace having been given to me, Iakobos and Kephas and Ioannes, the [ones] reputed to be pillars, gave to me and Barnabas [the] right [hands] of fellowship, that we [should go] to the nations and they to the circumcision;

10. only that we should remember the poor, which very thing I was also eager to do.

11. But when Kephas came to Antiocheia, I opposed him to [his] face, because he stood condemned.

12. For before certain [ones] came from Iakobos, he was eating with the nations; but when they came, he was drawing back and separating himself, fearing the [ones] of [the] circumcision.

13. And the rest of the Ioudaioi also joined in hypocrisy with him, so that even Barnabas was carried away by their hypocrisy.

14. But when I saw that they were not walking straight toward the truth of the gospel, I said to Kephas before all: "If you, being a Ioudaios, live like the nations and not like a Ioudaios, how do you compel the nations to live like Ioudaioi?"

15. We [are] Ioudaioi by nature and not sinners from [the] nations;

16. but knowing that a man is not justified from works of law, except through faith of Yeshua Christos, even we believed into Christos Yeshua, that we might be justified from faith of Christos and not from works of law, because from works of law no flesh will be justified.

17. But if, seeking to be justified in Christos, we ourselves also were found [to be] sinners, is Christos then a servant of sin? May it never be!

18. For if I build up again the [things] which I tore down, I demonstrate myself [to be] a transgressor.

19. For I through law died to law, that I might live to God.

20. I have been crucified with Christos. And it is no longer I [who] live, but Christos lives in me. And the [life] which I now live in [the] flesh, I live by faith — the [faith] of the Son of God, the [one] having loved me and having given himself up for me.

21. I do not set aside the grace of God; for if righteousness [is] through law, then Christos died for nothing.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.2 — "according to a revelation":** kata apokalypsin — Paul's second visit to Jerusalem was prompted by divine revelation, not human summons. This reinforces his independence from the Jerusalem authorities.

2. **v.4 — "false brothers":** pseudadelphous — a compound word: pseudo + adelphos. These are infiltrators within the community, not external opponents.

3. **v.6 — "God does not receive the face of a man":** prosopon anthropou theos ou lambanei — a Hebraism meaning "God does not show partiality." The idiom "receive the face" (Hebrew: nasa panim) means to be swayed by someone's status or appearance.

4. **v.9 — "pillars":** styloi — a term of architectural metaphor. James, Cephas, and John are the structural supports of the Jerusalem community. The term may be connected to the Temple's pillars.

5. **v.16 — "faith of Christos":** pisteos Christou — the genitive is ambiguous: "faith in Christ" (objective genitive) or "the faithfulness of Christ" (subjective genitive). I preserve the genitive construction literally: "faith of Christ." This is one of the most debated grammatical questions in Pauline studies.

6. **v.20 — "I have been crucified with Christos":** Christō synestaurōmai — perfect passive: the crucifixion-with-Christ is a completed event with ongoing results. The "I" that lives is paradoxically no longer the old "I" — Christ lives in Paul. This is the theological center of the letter.

— EMET, AI Translator
